perturbation.original_text: The Suppliers shall notify the Company of acceptance or rejection of a [***]within [***]hours of receipt of a [***]. Failure of the Suppliers to accept or reject a [***]within [***]hours shall constitute acceptance of such [***].
perturbation.changed_text: The Suppliers should endeavor to notify the Company of acceptance or rejection of a [***] within a reasonable timeframe of receipt of a [***]. Failure of the Suppliers to respond in what they consider a reasonable time may constitute acceptance of such [***].
perturbation.explanation: The original text sets a clear deadline for acceptance or rejection of a purchase order. The modified text introduces ambiguity by using phrases like 'should endeavor,' 'reasonable timeframe,' and 'what they consider a reasonable time,' making the obligation less definite and more open to interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-206(1)(a) states that an offer to make a contract shall be construed as inviting acceptance in any manner and by any medium reasonable in the circumstances. By introducing subjective terms like 'reasonable timeframe' and allowing the supplier to define 'reasonable,' the modification contradicts the UCC's goal of providing clear rules for contract formation and acceptance, potentially leading to disputes over whether a contract was actually formed due to the vague response deadline.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-206. Offer and Acceptance in Formation of Contract. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-206. Offer and Acceptance in Formation of Contract. (1) Unless otherwise unambiguously indicated by the language or circumstances (a) an offer to make a contract shall be construed as inviting acceptance in any manner and by any medium reasonable in the circumstances; (b) an order or other offer to buy goods for prompt or current shipment shall be construed as inviting acceptance either by a prompt promise to ship or by the prompt or current shipment of conforming or non-conforming goods, but such a shipment of non-conforming goods does not constitute an acceptance if the seller seasonably notifies the buyer that the shipment is offered only as an accommodation to the buyer. (2) Where the beginning of a requested performance is a reasonable mode of acceptance an offeror who is not notified of acceptance within a reasonable time may treat the offer as having lapsed before acceptance. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by using phrases like 'should endeavor,' 'reasonable timeframe,' and 'what they consider a reasonable time,' which contradicts the original text's clear deadline for acceptance or rejection. This shift towards subjective interpretation undermines the clarity sought by UCC § 2-206(1)(a), which aims to construe offers as inviting acceptance in any manner and by any medium reasonable in the circumstances. The change makes the obligation less definite and more open to dispute, creating a legal contradiction by deviating from the UCC's goal of clear contract formation.

perturbation.original_text: Suppliers shall deliver the Product to the Company in accordance with the Delivery Term, shipping instructions in the Purchase Order issued by the Company with regard to the requested delivery date (subject to the Product Lead Time), ship-to address, and carrier.
perturbation.changed_text: Suppliers will attempt to deliver the Product to the Company in general accordance with the Delivery Term and will consider the shipping instructions in the Purchase Order issued by the Company, bearing in mind the requested delivery date, ship-to address, and carrier.
perturbation.explanation: The original text establishes a firm obligation for the Suppliers to deliver according to the specified terms. The modified text weakens this obligation by using phrases like 'will attempt,' 'in general accordance,' and 'bearing in mind,' which introduce discretion and make it less enforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-309 requires that the time for shipment or delivery, if not otherwise agreed, shall be a reasonable time. The added language allows the supplier too much leeway and does not ensure a 'reasonable time' for delivery will be met and enforced. It also undermines the definiteness required for delivery terms under the UCC, increasing the risk of disputes over timely performance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the supplier's delivery obligations, which contradicts the principle of 'reasonable time' for delivery as outlined in UCC § 2-309. The original text established a 'shall' obligation, but the changed text uses phrases like 'will attempt' and 'in general accordance,' weakening the enforceability of the delivery terms. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that 'the time for shipment or delivery...if not...agreed upon shall be a reasonable time.' By making the delivery terms vague, the modified contract fails to ensure a 'reasonable time' as mandated by the UCC, creating a contradiction and increasing the risk of disputes. The Perturbation Explanation also indicates that the new language introduces discretion, while the Law Explanation confirms the undermining of definiteness required for delivery terms under the UCC.

perturbation.original_text: Each of the Suppliers warrants that the Products shall comply with the specifications and documentation agreed by the relevant Supplier and the Company in writing that is applicable to such Products for the Warranty Period.
perturbation.changed_text: Each of the Suppliers generally warrants that the Products will, in their reasonable opinion, substantially comply with the specifications and documentation as they understand them to be agreed by the relevant Supplier and the Company that seems applicable to such Products for the Warranty Period.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a clear warranty obligation tied to agreed-upon specifications. The modified text introduces significant ambiguity through terms like 'generally warrants,' 'in their reasonable opinion,' 'substantially comply,' 'as they understand them to be agreed,' and 'seems applicable,' weakening the warranty and making it difficult to enforce specific standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-313 governs express warranties by affirmation, promise, description, or sample. This modification, by inserting subjective phrases like 'substantially comply' and 'in their reasonable opinion', contradicts the UCC's focus on objective standards for warranties, making it harder for the buyer to prove a breach of warranty. The language makes the warranty so vague that it's almost meaningless, defeating the purpose of an express warranty under the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. (1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows: (a) Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. (b) Any description of the goods which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the description. (c) Any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the whole of the goods shall conform to the sample or model. (2) It is not necessary to the creation of an express warranty that the seller use formal words such as "warrant" or "guarantee" or that he have a specific intention to make a warranty, but an affirmation merely of the value of the goods or a statement purporting to be merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the goods does not create a warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by using terms like 'generally warrants,' 'in their reasonable opinion,' and 'substantially comply.' This directly contradicts the principles of UCC § 2-313, which emphasizes objective standards for express warranties. The original text provides a clear standard tied to agreed-upon specifications. The altered text weakens the enforceability of the warranty by making it subjective and dependent on the supplier's interpretation, making it harder for the buyer to prove a breach. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that express warranties are created by 'Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise.' The modifications weaken this affirmation and promise, creating a contradiction.
